title: Interests, Positions and BATNA in Engineering Negotiations
description: Learn how to distinguish between positions and interests, assess alternatives, develop a strong BATNA and use these concepts to prepare and conduct negotiations in engineering projects.

sections.title: Learning Objectives
items: Explain the difference between a position and an interest.
Identify underlying interests behind stated demands.
Distinguish between interests, priorities, constraints and preferences.
Explain what BATNA means and why it is important.
Develop and improve a BATNA before a negotiation.
Recognize the difference between BATNA and reservation point.
Use alternatives to evaluate proposed agreements.
Avoid common mistakes when assessing bargaining power.
Apply interest-based negotiation to engineering project scenarios.
Use questions and information analysis to discover the other party's interests.
Evaluate how uncertainty affects BATNA and decision-making.
Develop agreements that satisfy important interests without accepting every stated position.

sections.title: Why Interests, Positions and BATNA Matter
content: Engineering negotiations often appear to involve conflicting demands.
A contractor asks for more time.
A client rejects a price increase.
A supplier demands earlier payment.
A project manager requires a particular technical solution.
These demands are positions, but they do not always reveal what the parties truly need.
Understanding interests and alternatives allows negotiators to move beyond fixed demands.
This can lead to more practical, balanced and implementable agreements.

sections.title: What Is a Position?
content: A position is the outcome or demand a party states during a negotiation.
It describes what the party says it wants.
Positions are often expressed as specific prices, dates, responsibilities or contract terms.
Examples include demanding EUR 200,000, requesting ten additional days or refusing liability for delay.
A position may be firm, flexible, strategic or only an opening proposal.
A stated position does not necessarily represent the party's final limit.

sections.title: Examples of Positions
items: The contractor requires a six-week extension.
The buyer will not pay more than EUR 500,000.
The supplier requires payment within fourteen days.
The client insists on a five-year warranty.
The project manager refuses to change the testing procedure.
The shipyard requires unrestricted access to the vessel.
The owner rejects responsibility for additional design work.

sections.title: What Is an Interest?
content: An interest is the underlying reason, need, concern or motivation behind a position.
Interests explain why a particular outcome matters.
A party may have financial, technical, operational, legal, relational or reputational interests.
Several different positions may satisfy the same interest.
Identifying interests therefore creates opportunities for alternative solutions.

sections.title: Position and Underlying Interest
content: A contractor requests six additional weeks.
The underlying interest may be avoiding liquidated damages.
It may also be securing enough time to complete the work safely.
The contractor may be concerned about resource shortages or delayed design information.
Once the interest is understood, the parties can evaluate other solutions.
These may include partial extensions, additional resources, revised sequencing or milestone adjustments.

sections.title: Positions Are Visible, Interests Are Often Hidden
content: Positions are normally communicated directly.
Interests may not be obvious.
A party may avoid revealing its interests because of confidentiality or bargaining concerns.
The negotiator may also be uncertain about its own interests.
Some interests are organizational rather than personal.
Others may relate to internal approval, budget cycles, risk policies or stakeholder expectations.

sections.title: Types of Interests
items: Financial interests, such as cost control, profit, cash flow and budget compliance.
Technical interests, such as safety, reliability, compatibility and performance.
Schedule interests, such as meeting milestones or protecting operational availability.
Legal interests, such as compliance, liability and enforceability.
Commercial interests, such as payment terms, warranty and risk allocation.
Operational interests, such as maintainability, access and spare-parts availability.
Relational interests, such as trust, cooperation and future business.
Reputational interests, such as protecting credibility with customers or authorities.
Procedural interests, such as being consulted and treated fairly.

sections.title: Substantive and Procedural Interests
content: Substantive interests concern the actual outcome of the negotiation.
They include price, time, quality, scope and responsibility.
Procedural interests concern how the negotiation and decision process is managed.
A party may care about transparency, equal treatment, documentation or the opportunity to be heard.
Ignoring procedural interests can create resistance even when the substantive proposal is reasonable.

sections.title: Procedural Interest in a Project
content: An engineering subcontractor rejects a proposed design change.
The technical solution itself may be acceptable.
However, the subcontractor believes it was excluded from the design process.
Its resistance may therefore be connected to recognition, consultation and responsibility.
Including the subcontractor in the technical review may reduce opposition.

sections.title: Shared, Different and Conflicting Interests
content: Not all interests are directly opposed.
The parties may have shared interests, such as safety, successful completion and avoidance of disputes.
They may have different interests that are not incompatible.
For example, one party may value early payment while the other values a lower total price.
Some interests may be genuinely conflicting, such as how an unavoidable cost should be divided.
Good negotiators identify which category each issue belongs to.

sections.title: Shared Interests in a Dry-Docking Project
items: Completing work safely.
Returning the vessel to service on time.
Preventing technical failure.
Meeting class and regulatory requirements.
Avoiding unnecessary rework.
Maintaining accurate documentation.
Protecting the commercial relationship.

sections.title: Why Parties Become Trapped in Positions
content: Positions are easy to communicate and defend.
Once a position is stated publicly, changing it may appear to signal weakness.
Internal stakeholders may expect the negotiator to maintain a firm demand.
Emotions and previous conflicts may also make positions more rigid.
The parties may begin arguing about who is right instead of exploring what the project needs.
This can create deadlock even when a workable solution exists.

sections.title: The Risk of Positional Bargaining
items: The parties may defend extreme demands.
Important information may not be shared.
Concessions may be interpreted as weakness.
The negotiation may become personal.
Project problems may remain unresolved.
The final compromise may not address the underlying interests.
The agreement may be difficult to implement.

sections.title: Moving from Positions to Interests
content: The negotiator should ask why the stated position matters.
Questions should be respectful and neutral.
The objective is not to challenge the other party's legitimacy.
It is to understand the problem that the position is intended to solve.
Once interests are identified, the parties can create and compare alternative solutions.

sections.title: Questions That Reveal Interests
items: What is the main concern behind this requirement?
Which part of the proposal is most important to you?
What problem would this term solve?
What would happen if the requested date could not be achieved?
Which risks are you trying to avoid?
What internal approval requirements apply?
What alternatives have you considered?
Which parts of your proposal are flexible?

sections.title: Listening for Interests
content: Interests are not discovered only through direct questions.
They may appear in repeated concerns, objections or explanations.
A negotiator should listen for references to risk, deadlines, customers, budgets and internal stakeholders.
Changes in language can also provide clues.
For example, a party may repeatedly focus on certainty rather than price.
This may indicate that predictability is the dominant interest.

sections.title: Do Not Assume the Other Party's Interests
content: Negotiators often assume they know why the other party holds a position.
These assumptions may be incorrect.
A supplier requesting early payment may not be financially weak.
It may be following a corporate cash-management policy.
A contractor requesting additional time may not lack resources.
The delay may result from access restrictions or approval procedures.
Interests should therefore be explored and verified.

sections.title: Interests and Priorities
content: Not all interests are equally important.
Before negotiating, a party should rank its interests.
Some requirements may be essential.
Others may be desirable but flexible.
Low-priority issues may be exchanged for progress on high-priority issues.
Priority analysis is necessary for effective trade-offs.

sections.title: Priority Analysis
items: Essential: The equipment must satisfy classification requirements.
High priority: Delivery should occur before the planned dry dock.
Medium priority: The warranty should exceed the standard period.
Low priority: Training may be provided remotely rather than onboard.
Flexible: The reporting format may follow the supplier's standard template.

sections.title: Interests and Constraints
content: An interest describes what a party wants to protect or achieve.
A constraint limits what the party can accept or deliver.
Constraints may include laws, technical limitations, budgets, resources or approval rules.
A genuine constraint should be distinguished from a preference.
Understanding constraints prevents the parties from negotiating unrealistic solutions.

sections.title: Interest, Preference and Constraint
content: A client prefers delivery in September.
Its underlying interest is completing installation before winter operations.
A class survey scheduled in October creates a practical deadline.
The preference may be flexible, while the operational and regulatory constraints may not be.
The negotiation should focus on the real limitation rather than the preferred date alone.

sections.title: Generating Options from Interests
content: Once interests are understood, the parties should develop several options.
The first solution should not automatically become the final agreement.
Options may involve changes to price, scope, sequence, responsibility, timing or risk allocation.
The parties should first create possibilities and then evaluate them.
This reduces the risk that criticism prevents useful ideas from emerging.

sections.title: Options for a Delivery Conflict
content: The supplier cannot deliver the complete system by the required date.
The client's interest is avoiding disruption to commissioning.
Possible options include partial delivery, temporary equipment, air freight for critical components or revised installation sequencing.
The supplier may also provide additional technicians or remote support.
Several options may protect the client's interest without requiring full delivery by the original date.

sections.title: What Is BATNA?
content: BATNA means Best Alternative to a Negotiated Agreement.
It is the best course of action available if no agreement is reached.
BATNA is not the preferred negotiated outcome.
It is the strongest realistic alternative outside the current agreement.
A negotiator uses BATNA to evaluate whether a proposed agreement is better than walking away.

sections.title: Simple BATNA Example
content: A company is negotiating with Supplier A.
If no agreement is reached, the company can purchase equivalent equipment from Supplier B.
Supplier B has a higher price but can meet the technical requirements.
Purchasing from Supplier B may therefore be the company's BATNA.
Any agreement with Supplier A should normally be compared with that alternative.

sections.title: Why BATNA Creates Negotiation Discipline
content: Without a BATNA, a party may feel forced to accept an unfavourable agreement.
A clear alternative reduces emotional pressure.
It provides a reference point for evaluating proposals.
BATNA also helps prevent agreement for agreement's sake.
The objective is not always to reach a deal.
The objective is to reach a deal that is better than the available alternatives.

sections.title: BATNA Is Not a Threat
content: A BATNA exists outside the negotiation.
It should not be confused with a threat made to pressure the other party.
The negotiator may communicate that alternatives exist without using aggressive language.
A threat can damage trust and may not be credible.
A strong BATNA provides confidence even when it is not disclosed.

sections.title: How to Develop a BATNA
items: Identify what happens if no agreement is reached.
List all realistic alternatives.
Assess the cost, time, risk and feasibility of each alternative.
Improve the most promising alternatives where possible.
Select the best realistic alternative.
Compare negotiated proposals against that alternative.
Update the analysis when circumstances change.

sections.title: Developing a BATNA for Equipment Procurement
content: A project team is negotiating with its preferred pump supplier.
The team identifies alternative suppliers, equipment redesign and delayed procurement as possible alternatives.
Each option is assessed for price, lead time, technical risk and project impact.
A second supplier can meet the requirements with a three-week longer delivery time.
The team investigates whether the schedule can absorb the delay.
This makes the second supplier a more credible alternative.

sections.title: Improving Your BATNA
content: BATNA is not always fixed.
A party can often improve its alternatives before or during a negotiation.
The client may qualify additional suppliers.
The contractor may secure alternative resources.
The project team may redesign a component or change the sequence of work.
Improving alternatives increases flexibility and bargaining strength.

sections.title: Improving an Alternative
content: A shipowner depends on one service company for a specialized repair.
Before negotiating, the owner contacts other approved companies.
One company cannot perform the entire repair but can provide specialist personnel.
The owner also confirms that spare parts are available independently.
These preparations improve the owner's alternatives and reduce dependency.

sections.title: A BATNA Must Be Realistic
content: A theoretical possibility is not automatically a valid BATNA.
The alternative must be feasible within relevant time, cost and technical constraints.
A supplier is not a meaningful alternative if it lacks capacity or approval.
Legal action is not necessarily a strong BATNA if it is expensive, uncertain and slow.
The quality of BATNA analysis depends on realistic evidence.

sections.title: Common BATNA Errors
items: Treating an unrealistic option as a credible alternative.
Ignoring the cost of switching suppliers.
Overestimating legal rights or claim value.
Underestimating project delay.
Failing to consider implementation risk.
Assuming that an alternative remains available indefinitely.
Confusing the preferred solution with the fallback option.
Failing to update the BATNA when conditions change.

sections.title: BATNA and Bargaining Power
content: A strong BATNA normally increases bargaining power.
A party that can walk away at limited cost is less dependent on the agreement.
However, bargaining power also depends on the other party's alternatives.
The relative strength of both BATNAs matters.
Power may change as the project progresses and switching becomes more difficult.

sections.title: Changing Power During a Project
content: Before contract award, the client has four qualified suppliers.
After selecting one supplier, the design is adapted to its equipment.
Changing supplier would then require redesign, delay and additional approval.
The client's BATNA becomes weaker.
The supplier's bargaining position may therefore improve during project execution.

sections.title: Assessing the Other Party's BATNA
content: A negotiator should estimate the other party's alternatives.
This helps explain its level of flexibility and bargaining power.
The analysis should consider market conditions, capacity, deadlines and switching costs.
The other party's BATNA is rarely known with certainty.
It should therefore be treated as an informed estimate rather than a fact.

sections.title: Estimating a Supplier's BATNA
content: A supplier has limited production capacity for the next six months.
The client knows that the supplier is discussing another large order.
If the current negotiation fails, the supplier may allocate capacity to another customer.
This may strengthen the supplier's BATNA.
However, the client should verify market information before changing its strategy.

sections.title: Questions for Assessing the Other Party's Alternatives
items: What happens if no agreement is reached?
Does the other party have alternative customers or suppliers?
How expensive would switching be?
Is the other party under time pressure?
Are specialist resources available elsewhere?
What approvals or contractual restrictions apply?
How important is this agreement to the other party?
Could the issue be postponed or solved internally?

sections.title: BATNA versus Reservation Point
content: BATNA and reservation point are related but different.
BATNA is the best alternative if no agreement is reached.
The reservation point is the least favourable negotiated outcome a party is prepared to accept.
The reservation point should reflect the value and risk of the BATNA.
An agreement worse than the reservation point should normally be rejected.

sections.title: BATNA and Reservation Point Example
content: A buyer can purchase equipment from an alternative supplier for EUR 620,000.
Switching would also create EUR 30,000 in redesign cost and some schedule risk.
The total value of the BATNA is therefore worse than the purchase price alone suggests.
The buyer may set a reservation point for the preferred supplier based on this full comparison.
The exact limit should include cost, time, quality and risk.

sections.title: BATNA Is More Than a Price
content: Engineering alternatives often involve several dimensions.
A lower-cost alternative may create longer delivery time.
A faster option may involve greater technical risk.
An alternative supplier may offer weaker warranty or support.
BATNA should therefore be evaluated using total project impact.
Price alone is rarely sufficient.

sections.title: Multidimensional BATNA Analysis
items: Purchase price.
Engineering and redesign cost.
Delivery and schedule impact.
Technical compliance.
Quality and reliability.
Warranty and service.
Switching and mobilization cost.
Operational consequences.
Contractual and legal risk.
Relationship impact.

sections.title: BATNA under Uncertainty
content: Alternatives are often uncertain.
A replacement supplier may not confirm delivery until later.
A legal claim may have an uncertain outcome.
A technical workaround may not perform as expected.
The negotiator should assess probability and consequence.
Uncertainty should reduce the assumed value of an alternative.

sections.title: Risk-Adjusted Alternative
content: A project can reject a settlement and pursue arbitration.
The claim value is EUR 1 million.
However, success is uncertain, legal costs are high and the process may take two years.
The claim value cannot therefore be treated as a guaranteed EUR 1 million BATNA.
The alternative should be evaluated using expected cost, probability, delay and management effort.

sections.title: When to Reveal a BATNA
content: A party does not normally need to reveal its BATNA.
Disclosure may be useful if the alternative is strong, credible and likely to improve the negotiation.
The information should be communicated factually rather than as a threat.
A weak BATNA should normally remain confidential.
False claims about alternatives can damage credibility and trust.

sections.title: Professional BATNA Communication
content: A buyer may state that several technically qualified suppliers remain under consideration.
The buyer does not need to disclose confidential prices or its exact reservation point.
The message communicates that alternatives exist.
It also preserves commercial confidentiality.
The statement should be accurate and supportable.

sections.title: Do Not Bluff Carelessly
content: Claiming to have strong alternatives may create pressure.
However, the other party may test the claim or allow the negotiation to fail.
A false BATNA can leave the negotiator without an acceptable option.
Credibility is particularly important in long-term engineering relationships.

sections.title: Using BATNA during Negotiation
content: BATNA should guide decisions throughout the negotiation.
New proposals should be compared with the alternative.
Concessions should not reduce the agreement below the reservation point.
If the proposed agreement remains worse than the BATNA, the party should consider walking away.
If the agreement is better, the party should still try to improve it where possible.

sections.title: Walking Away
content: Walking away is sometimes the correct outcome.
The existence of negotiation does not create an obligation to agree.
A party should withdraw when no available agreement is better than its BATNA.
The decision should be based on preparation rather than anger or pride.
The consequences of ending the negotiation should be understood before doing so.

sections.title: When Rejection May Be Rational
content: A repair contractor offers a price that is lower than alternatives.
However, it refuses to guarantee completion before the vessel's scheduled departure.
The owner has another contractor that costs more but can provide a reliable completion date.
The lower price may not compensate for the operational risk.
Rejecting the cheaper offer may therefore be rational.

sections.title: Interests and BATNA Work Together
content: Interests explain what the party wants from an agreement.
BATNA explains what the party can do without the agreement.
Both are necessary for effective preparation.
Interests help negotiators create value.
BATNA helps them protect value.
A strong negotiation process combines creative problem-solving with disciplined limits.

sections.title: Using Interests to Strengthen Alternatives
content: BATNA development should begin with the party's real interests.
An alternative does not need to reproduce the preferred solution exactly.
It only needs to protect the important interests sufficiently.
For example, the interest may be operational continuity rather than ownership of a specific component.
A temporary rental solution may therefore be an effective alternative.

sections.title: Alternative Solution Based on Interests
content: A company wants to purchase a new generator before a planned shutdown.
The supplier cannot meet the required delivery date.
The company's main interest is maintaining backup power.
Its BATNA may be renting a temporary generator rather than purchasing from another supplier.
The alternative protects the operational interest while allowing more time for procurement.

sections.title: Multiple Stakeholder Interests
content: Engineering negotiations often involve more than two parties.
The project manager may represent the client, but operations, finance, legal and technical teams may have different interests.
External stakeholders may include authorities, class societies, customers and insurers.
The negotiator should identify internal interests before meeting the other party.
Unresolved internal disagreement can weaken the negotiation position.

sections.title: Internal Interests in a Procurement Negotiation
items: Engineering wants proven technical performance.
Procurement wants competitive price.
Operations wants easy maintenance.
Finance wants favourable payment terms.
Legal wants controlled liability.
Management wants schedule certainty.
The negotiator must balance these interests in one strategy.

sections.title: Internal Alignment before Negotiation
content: The negotiation team should agree on essential interests, priorities and limits.
Each team member should understand the BATNA.
The team should identify which issues can be traded.
Authority to make concessions should be clear.
Internal alignment reduces inconsistent messages and unplanned commitments.

sections.title: Internal Positions Can Also Create Deadlock
content: A technical department may insist on one design.
Procurement may insist on the lowest price.
Operations may reject any unfamiliar solution.
These internal positions may prevent the organization from evaluating better packages.
The team should discuss the interests behind each internal demand.

sections.title: Using Objective Criteria with Interests
content: Interests do not automatically determine which solution is fair.
Objective criteria help evaluate competing proposals.
Relevant criteria may include market prices, engineering calculations, standards and historical data.
They provide a neutral basis for comparing options.
This is especially useful when interests are legitimate but conflicting.

sections.title: Objective Criteria in a Delay Negotiation
content: The contractor wants additional time to avoid delay damages.
The client wants to protect the completion date.
The parties analyze the critical path, progress records and access delays.
The evidence identifies which events affected completion.
The final extension is based on schedule impact rather than bargaining pressure alone.

sections.title: Creating Trades from Different Interests
content: Negotiation becomes more productive when the parties value issues differently.
One party may prioritize price while the other prioritizes cash flow.
One may value schedule certainty while the other values resource flexibility.
These differences create opportunities for trades.
A package can satisfy high-priority interests at relatively low cost to the other party.

sections.title: Trade Based on Different Priorities
content: The supplier values early payment.
The client values extended warranty and faster technical response.
The client agrees to an earlier payment milestone.
In exchange, the supplier provides a longer warranty and guaranteed response time.
Both parties improve the value of the agreement.

sections.title: Avoiding Unnecessary Concessions
content: Understanding interests helps prevent unnecessary concessions.
A party may assume that the other side requires a lower price.
The real interest may be budget timing or payment structure.
Changing the payment schedule may solve the problem without reducing total value.
Negotiators should explore the interest before offering concessions.

sections.title: Interest-Based Negotiation Does Not Mean Agreement at Any Cost
content: Interest-based negotiation is sometimes misunderstood as being soft.
It does not require accepting unreasonable terms.
The negotiator should understand interests while protecting essential limits.
BATNA and reservation point remain important.
The approach combines cooperation in problem-solving with discipline in decision-making.

sections.title: Firm and Interest-Based
content: A contractor explains that increased material cost threatens the profitability of the work.
The client acknowledges the concern and reviews the evidence.
However, the contract clearly places ordinary price escalation risk on the contractor.
The client explores schedule and scope adjustments but does not accept the entire claim.
The approach is respectful, evidence-based and commercially firm.

sections.title: Emotions, Identity and Interests
content: Not all interests are financial or technical.
People may care about respect, recognition, control and professional credibility.
A negotiator who feels ignored may resist even a reasonable proposal.
Acknowledging concerns does not mean accepting the other party's position.
It can reduce defensiveness and support constructive dialogue.

sections.title: Recognition as an Interest
content: A senior engineer strongly opposes a design change.
Technical analysis shows that the change is acceptable.
The engineer may believe that previous work is being dismissed.
Inviting the engineer to review risks and define acceptance criteria may address the interest in professional recognition.
This can improve both the decision and the relationship.

sections.title: Documenting Interest-Based Agreements
content: A successful discussion must be converted into clear contractual or project commitments.
The agreement should state scope, price, schedule, responsibilities and approval requirements.
It should also explain any conditions or assumptions.
Goodwill and shared interests do not replace precise documentation.
Clear documentation protects implementation and reduces future disputes.

sections.title: BATNA after Agreement
content: BATNA remains relevant until the agreement is binding and implementable.
A verbal understanding may still require internal approval.
Conditions precedent may remain unresolved.
The alternative should not be abandoned too early.
A party should maintain important options until the agreement is sufficiently secure.

sections.title: Do Not Lose the BATNA Too Early
content: A buyer may stop discussions with alternative suppliers before the preferred contract is signed.
A contractor may release resources based only on an informal promise.
If the agreement later fails, the party may have no effective alternative.
Options should be closed carefully and deliberately.

sections.title: Updating BATNA during the Project
content: Alternatives change over time.
Supplier capacity may become unavailable.
Market prices may increase.
Technical information may reveal new solutions.
Project delay may make switching more expensive.
The negotiation team should update BATNA analysis before important decisions.

sections.title: Expert Insight
content: Strong negotiators do not choose between cooperation and firmness.
They use both.
They cooperate to understand interests and create better options.
They remain firm when evaluating those options against their BATNA and reservation point.
In engineering projects, this combination is essential because agreements must be technically feasible, commercially acceptable and operationally practical.
A deal that satisfies stated positions but ignores critical interests may fail during implementation.

sections.title: Common Mistakes
items: Treating positions as the same as interests.
Assuming the other party's motivations without asking questions.
Focusing only on price.
Failing to rank internal priorities.
Confusing preferences with genuine constraints.
Entering negotiation without a realistic BATNA.
Overestimating weak or uncertain alternatives.
Revealing the reservation point unnecessarily.
Using BATNA as an aggressive threat.
Failing to assess the other party's alternatives.
Accepting an agreement worse than the BATNA.
Abandoning alternatives before the agreement is secure.
Ignoring stakeholder interests within the negotiation team.
Failing to document the final solution clearly.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
items: A position is what a party says it wants.
An interest is the reason why that outcome matters.
Several positions may satisfy the same underlying interest.
Interests may be financial, technical, operational, legal or relational.
Shared and different interests can create opportunities for agreement.
Questions and active listening help reveal interests.
BATNA is the best alternative available if no agreement is reached.
A strong BATNA increases flexibility and bargaining power.
A BATNA must be realistic and evaluated using total project impact.
The reservation point is the least favourable agreement a party will accept.
The other party's BATNA should also be estimated.
BATNA and interests should be updated as circumstances change.
Interest-based negotiation does not mean accepting unfavourable terms.
The strongest approach combines creative value creation with disciplined decision-making.

sections.title: Reflection Questions
questions: What is the difference between a position and an interest?
Why can two apparently conflicting positions still allow agreement?
Which types of interests are common in engineering projects?
How can a negotiator discover the interests behind a demand?
Why should interests be ranked before negotiation?
What is the difference between an interest and a constraint?
Why must BATNA be realistic rather than theoretical?
How can a party improve its BATNA?
What is the difference between BATNA and reservation point?
Why should technical risk be included in BATNA analysis?
How can the other party's alternatives affect bargaining power?
When may it be useful to reveal that you have an alternative?
Why should a party sometimes reject a negotiated agreement?
How can interests and BATNA be used together in project negotiations?
